24.notes:
2 Samuel - Chapter 24
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
David sinfully numbers the people, and God sends a plague that kills 70,000. David buys the threshing floor of Araunah to build an altar, and the plague stops—this site will become the Temple mount.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Nathan and Gad
(1 Chronicles 29:29). Originally one book with 1 Samuel in the
Hebrew Bible. The book covers David's entire reign — from his
lament over Saul's death to the purchase of the threshing floor
for the future Temple (approximately 1010-970 BC). Key themes:
the Davidic covenant (chapter 7), the consequences of sin even
for the forgiven (chapters 11-20), God's faithfulness despite
human failure, and the establishment of the eternal throne
fulfilled in Christ.

Historical Context: Chapter 24 is the final chapter of 2 Samuel
and one of the most theologically complex passages in the Old
Testament. It records David's sin of numbering the people, the
resulting plague that kills 70,000, and David's purchase of
Araunah's threshing floor — the site where Solomon would later
build the Temple (2 Chronicles 3:1). The chapter raises profound
questions about divine sovereignty and human responsibility: the
text says God "moved David" (KJV) or "incited David" (ESV) to
take the census, yet David is held guilty for it. The parallel
account in 1 Chronicles 21:1 says "Satan stood up against Israel,
and provoked David to number Israel." Both are true: God
permitted Satan to tempt David, and David freely chose to yield.
The sin was not in counting people per se (Moses conducted
censuses by God's command in Numbers 1 and 26) but in David's
motive — pride, self-reliance, and trust in military strength
rather than in God. Even Joab recognized the sin and objected.
The chapter ends with mercy: God stops the plague at the
threshing floor, David purchases the site, offers sacrifice, and
the plague ceases. This location — Mount Moriah — is where
Abraham offered Isaac (Genesis 22:2) and where the Temple would
stand. The book of 2 Samuel thus ends where the Temple begins:
at the intersection of judgment, sacrifice, and mercy.

David's Census — The Sin of Pride (vv.1-9): God's anger burns
against Israel (the reason is not stated — perhaps accumulated
national sin), and He permits David to be tempted to number the
people. David commands Joab to conduct a military census "from
Dan even to Beer-sheba." Joab objects — recognizing this as
sinful pride — but David overrules him. The census takes nine
months and twenty days, covering the entire land. The result:
800,000 fighting men in Israel and 500,000 in Judah (1.3 million
total). The numbers themselves reveal the sin: David wanted to
know his military strength, trusting in numbers rather than God.

David's Conviction and the Three Choices (vv.10-14): David's
heart "smote him" — his conscience convicted him immediately
after the census was complete. He confesses: "I have sinned
greatly... I have done very foolishly" (v.10). God sends the
prophet Gad with three options for judgment: seven years of
famine (KJV; three years in ESV/Chronicles), three months
fleeing from enemies, or three days of pestilence. David's
response reveals profound theology: "Let us fall now into the
hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall
into the hand of man" (v.14). David chooses God's direct
judgment over human cruelty — trusting that even God's wrath is
tempered by mercy.

The Plague and God's Mercy (vv.15-17): The LORD sends pestilence,
and 70,000 die from Dan to Beersheba. When the destroying angel
reaches Jerusalem and stretches out his hand to destroy it, "the
LORD repented him of the evil" (KJV) / "the LORD relented from
the calamity" (ESV) and commands: "It is enough: stay now thine
hand." The angel stops at Araunah's threshing floor. David sees
the angel and intercedes: "These sheep, what have they done? Let
thine hand be against me" (v.17) — a shepherd's prayer,
offering himself for his flock.

The Altar and the Sacrifice (vv.18-25): Gad instructs David to
build an altar on Araunah's threshing floor. Araunah offers to
give David the site, the oxen, and the wood for free. David
refuses with the famous declaration: "Neither will I offer burnt
offerings unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me
nothing" (v.24). He pays fifty shekels of silver, builds the
altar, offers burnt offerings and peace offerings, and "the LORD
was intreated for the land, and the plague was stayed" (v.25).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- The census covers all Israel: "from Dan to Beer-sheba" — the full north-south extent of the kingdom
- The route: East across Jordan to Aroer (Gad), north to Dan, west to Sidon, south to Beer-sheba — a circuit of the entire land
- The threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite: On Mount Moriah in Jerusalem — the future site of Solomon's Temple

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/2-samuel-24/
  Guzik emphasizes the theological tension of verse 1 — God
  inciting David yet holding him responsible. He explains this
  through the lens of divine permission: God allowed Satan to
  tempt David as judgment on Israel's accumulated sin. Guzik also
  highlights the significance of the threshing floor location:
  where judgment stopped, the Temple would rise — making the
  place of near-destruction the place of perpetual mercy.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'I will not offer burnt offerings to the LORD my God of that
  which does cost me nothing.' Here is a royal word, worthy of
  the man after God's own heart. Cheap religion is worth exactly
  what it costs — nothing. That which costs us no sacrifice, no
  self-denial, no effort, no tears — what is it worth to God? He
  gave His only Son; shall we give Him our leftovers? David would
  not insult God with a costless offering. Neither should we. Let
  our worship, our service, our giving, our obedience cost us
  something — for only then does it truly honor the God who gave
  everything."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "he moved David against them" / ESV: "he incited David against them" — Hebrew "yasath" (from "suth") = to incite, move, entice. KJV "moved" is softer; ESV "incited" is stronger. The theological difficulty remains in both: God is the subject of the verb. The parallel in 1 Chronicles 21:1 has Satan as the subject, revealing that God permitted Satan to do the inciting. Both translations preserve the tension of the Hebrew text.
v.6              | KJV         | "the land of Tahtim-hodshi" / ESV: "Kadesh in the land of the Hittites" — The Hebrew here is obscure. KJV transliterates what appears to be a place name ("Tahtim- hodshi"), which is otherwise unknown. ESV follows the Septuagint and other ancient versions in reading "Kadesh in the land of the Hittites" — a known location that fits the geographic itinerary. This is likely a case of textual corruption in the Masoretic text that the ESV corrects from other witnesses.
v.13             | KJV         | "seven years of famine" / ESV: "three years of famine" — A significant numerical discrepancy. The KJV follows the Masoretic text ("seven"); the ESV follows the Septuagint and the parallel in 1 Chronicles 21:12 ("three"). "Three" fits the pattern better (three years, three months, three days — a descending scale of duration with increasing intensity). Most scholars consider "three" original and "seven" a scribal error.
v.16             | KJV         | "the LORD repented him of the evil" / ESV: "the LORD relented from the calamity" — Hebrew "nacham" = to be sorry, to relent, to change one's course of action. KJV "repented" can mislead modern readers into thinking God admitted moral fault. ESV "relented" better captures the meaning: God changed His course of action regarding the judgment. "Evil" (KJV) vs. "calamity" (ESV): Hebrew "ra'ah" can mean moral evil or calamity/disaster. Here it means the disaster of the plague, not moral evil — ESV is clearer.
v.23             | KJV         | "All these things did Araunah, as a king, give unto the king" / ESV: "All this, O king, Araunah gives to the king" — The Hebrew is ambiguous. KJV reads "as a king" — suggesting Araunah gave with royal generosity (or that he was formerly a Jebusite king). ESV reads "O king" as a vocative address to David. The textual tradition is divided; both readings are grammatically possible.
v.24             | KJV         | "fifty shekels of silver" / 1 Chronicles 21:25: "six hundred shekels of gold" — Though not a KJV/ESV difference (both say fifty shekels here), the discrepancy with Chronicles is notable. The likely explanation: David paid fifty shekels for the immediate threshing floor and oxen, while the larger sum in Chronicles represents the purchase of the entire surrounding site for the Temple complex.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Census/           | Hebrew "manah" = to count, number. A military                                     | 
Numbering         | census was not inherently sinful (Numbers 1:2), but David's census was motivated by pride and self-reliance — wanting to know his military strength apart from trust in God. The sin was in the motive, not the method. | 
Threshing         | A flat, elevated area of exposed rock or hard-                                    | 
Floor             | packed earth where grain was separated from chaff by oxen dragging sledges over it. Threshing floors were typically on hilltops to catch the wind for winnowing. Araunah's threshing floor was on Mount Moriah — the highest point in Jerusalem — making it the ideal site for the Temple. | 
Pestilence        | Hebrew "deber" = plague, epidemic disease. One of God's instruments of judgment throughout the Old Testament (Exodus 9:3, Leviticus 26:25). The plague here killed 70,000 in three days — a devastating judgment that demonstrated both God's power and, when He stopped it, His mercy. | 
Araunah           | Also called Ornan (1 Chronicles 21:15). A (Ornan)          Jebusite — a member of the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem. His threshing floor on Mount Moriah became the site of Solomon's Temple. That a Gentile's property became the location of God's house foreshadows the inclusion of all nations in God's plan of salvation. | 
Burnt             | Hebrew "olah" = that which ascends. A sacrifice                                   | 
Offering         completely consumed by fire on the altar | nothing kept back for the worshiper. It represented total dedication and atonement. David also offered "peace offerings" (shelamim) — sacrifices of fellowship and thanksgiving. Together they represent complete atonement and restored relationship. | KJV term

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Pride is subtle and deadly (vv.1-4). David's sin was not obvious
   immorality but quiet pride — wanting to count his military strength,
   trusting in numbers rather than God. Even Joab saw the danger. The most
   dangerous sins are often the most respectable: self-reliance, desire for
   control, trust in resources rather than in God. Examine your heart for
   the pride that masquerades as prudence.

2. Quick repentance is the mark of a godly heart (v.10). David's heart
   "smote him" immediately. He did not wait for a prophet to confront him
   (as with Bathsheba) — his own conscience convicted him. Spiritual
   maturity is measured not by the absence of sin but by the speed of
   repentance. When the Holy Spirit convicts, respond immediately.

3. God's mercy is greater than His judgment (v.14). David chose to fall into
   God's hands rather than man's — because he knew God's character. Even in
   wrath, God remembers mercy (Habakkuk 3:2). This is not presumption but
   faith: trusting that the God who judges is also the God who saves. His
   mercy is not weakness; it is His glory.

4. True worship costs something (v.24). David refused a free offering.
   Worship that costs nothing honors nothing. This challenges our
   comfortable Christianity: Do we give God our best or our leftovers? Do we
   sacrifice time, comfort, money, and reputation for Him — or only what is
   convenient? The cross cost God everything; our response should reflect
   that.

5. Where judgment stops, mercy builds (vv.16, 25). The plague stopped at
   Araunah's threshing floor. On that exact spot, Solomon built the Temple —
   the place where God's mercy would be dispensed for generations through
   sacrifice. God transforms places of judgment into places of grace. The
   cross is the ultimate example: the place of God's wrath became the place
   of the world's salvation.
